Evaluating Numerical Fidelity in Financial Documents: A Comparative Analysis of Naive and Table-Aware Dual-Branch RAG Architectures

A Capstone Project in Data Science

Published

June 20, 2026

Slides: slides.html ( Go to slides.qmd to edit)

Introduction

The Real-World Risk: Why Tiny AI Mistakes Matter

Today, many companies use AI assistants to quickly find information buried in their internal reports. These systems are great at summarizing emails or articles, but they often fail when reading financial tables (Guo, Geng, and Mao 2026). Imagine an employee asking the AI system for a company’s total debt from a dense financial document. In a typical financial table, a number like “450” might mean $450 million if it is listed under a header that says “figures in millionsthousands.” Because standard AI reads text in a straight line from left to right, it can lose track of those important headers and labels. It might report the debt as just $450 or link the number to the wrong year entirely. In the world of finance, a mistake like this isn’t just a small typo; it can lead to massive losses or legal trouble.

This problem is commonly known as the gap between what a table says and what the AI actually understands. The dominant approach for building document-querying AI systems is Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) (Malali 2025) — a method that retrieves relevant passages from a document library before generating a response, grounding the AI’s answer in real source material. While RAG has proven effective for regulatory compliance, audit reporting, and other text-heavy financial tasks, its standard pipeline was designed for narrative prose and breaks down when applied to structured tables. This paper compares a standard RAG baseline against a table-aware pipeline on a corpus of real financial reports, introducing a Numerical Atomic Verification (NAV) metric to explicitly measure numerical fidelity in model outputs.

The Core Problem Statement: The Structural Evidence Gap

The core issue is that standard AI systems struggle to read complex financial tables. While these systems are great at reading plain text, they fail when processing financial data because they try to parse tables line by line, as if they were sentences (Guo, Geng, and Mao 2026). We refer to this as the Structural Evidence Gap. This leads to two main problems:

  1. Context Loss: When the AI system breaks text into smaller chunks, it separates numbers from their labels (like headers or rows). This means the AI loses track of what the numbers actually represent.
  2. Formatting Issues: Because standard tools read tables linearly, they jumble the data, mixing up different rows and columns.

While these AI systems work well for general reading, they fail when analyzing complex financial records in which answers are hidden across multiple structured tables.

Overview of Approach: Table-Aware Dual-Branch RAG

To solve the problems described above, this paper compares two approaches:

  • Architecture A (Naive RAG): This standard method uses simple linear PDF reading and basic semantic search, which often misses the structure of financial tables.
  • Architecture B (Table-Aware Dual-Branch RAG): This is our proposed solution, which helps retain structural lineage between document context and table information.

Our approach includes three key steps:

  1. Bifurcated Ingestion: The system separates narrative text from tabular data at the beginning, ensuring tables are preserved as structured data rather than flattened into plain text.
  2. Hybrid Indexing: We store each table row as an individual record, enriched with its column/row labels (Liu and Leong 2026). This ensures the AI system always knows exactly what a number represents (e.g., Fiscal Year 2025 vs. “2025”).
  3. Numerical Atomic Verification (NAV): Before the system provides an answer, a final verification step automatically checks all numbers against the original source document. If a discrepancy or unit mismatch is found, it is flagged to ensure absolute mathematical accuracy.

The pipeline is benchmarked against standard industry metrics to prove its effectiveness in handling complex financial documents.

Methods

Analysis and Results

Data Exploration and Visualization

The dataset for this project consists of Annual Financial Statements from the University of West Florida. These documents are highly complex, containing dense narrative prose mixed with structured financial tables. Using our custom ingestion pipeline (src/analytics.py), we extracted metadata about the structural composition of the documents before passing them into the RAG vector index.

Code 
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# Load data
df = pd.read_csv('data/corpus_statistics.csv')

# Set plot style
sns.set_theme(style="whitegrid")

# 1. Corpus Volume & Complexity
fig, ax1 = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))

df_melted = df.melt(id_vars='Fiscal_Year', value_vars=['Total_Pages', 'Total_Text_Blocks'], 
                    var_name='Metric', value_name='Count')

sns.barplot(data=df_melted, x='Fiscal_Year', y='Count', hue='Metric', palette='viridis', ax=ax1)
ax1.set_title('Document Complexity: Total Pages vs Text Blocks', fontsize=14)
ax1.set_xlabel('Fiscal Year')
ax1.set_ylabel('Count')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
Code 
# 2. Tabular Density Analysis
fig, ax2 = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.lineplot(data=df, x='Fiscal_Year', y='Total_Tables', marker='o', label='Total Tables', color='blue', linewidth=2, ax=ax2)
ax2_twin = ax2.twinx()
sns.lineplot(data=df, x='Fiscal_Year', y='Total_Table_Rows', marker='s', label='Total Table Rows', color='red', linewidth=2, ax=ax2_twin)

ax2.set_title('Tabular Density over Fiscal Years', fontsize=14)
ax2.set_xlabel('Fiscal Year')
ax2.set_ylabel('Number of Tables', color='blue')
ax2_twin.set_ylabel('Number of Rows', color='red')

# Combine legends
lines, labels = ax2.get_legend_handles_labels()
lines2, labels2 = ax2_twin.get_legend_handles_labels()
ax2_twin.legend(lines + lines2, labels + labels2, loc=0)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
Code 
# 3. Vector Index Composition (Pie Chart)
total_narrative = df['Narrative_Chunks'].sum()
total_srse = df['SRSE_Chunks'].sum()

fig, ax3 = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 8))
labels = ['Narrative Text Chunks', 'Structured Table Row Chunks (SRSE)']
sizes = [total_narrative, total_srse]
colors = sns.color_palette('pastel')[0:2]

ax3.pie(sizes, labels=labels, colors=colors, autopct='%1.1f%%', startangle=90, textprops={'fontsize': 12})
<matplotlib.container.PieContainer object at 0x000001594662B770>
Code 
ax3.axis('equal')  # Equal aspect ratio ensures that pie is drawn as a circle.
(np.float64(-1.0999994475428647), np.float64(1.0999999046601032), np.float64(-1.0999996791896884), np.float64(1.0999999847233184))
Code 
ax3.set_title('Overall Vector Index Data Composition', fontsize=14)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

References

Guo, Zebin, Weidong Geng, and Ruichen Mao. 2026. “FT-RAG: A Fine-Grained Retrieval-Augmented Generation Framework for Complex Table Reasoning.” In Proceedings of the 32nd ACM SIGKDD Conference on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining (KDD ’26). ACM.
Liu, Zhihao, and Waiyie Leong. 2026. “Table-Aware Row-Level RAG for Classical Chinese Understanding.” Computers 15 (4): 221. https://doi.org/10.3390/computers15040221.
Malali, N. 2025. “The Role of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) in Financial Document Processing.” International Journal of Management Technology 12 (3): 26–46. https://eajournals.org/ijmt/wp-content/uploads/sites/69/2025/04/The-Role-of-Retrieval-Augmented-Generation.pdf.
